quiz_info.sottotitolo: Discover how resistant you are to information manipulation techniques
quiz_info.istruzioni: For each situation, choose the response that best matches your real behaviour. At the end you'll see your score and personalised suggestions.

punteggio.0-8: 🦅 Guardian of Information – Critical and aware
punteggio.9-16: 🧐 Curious Investigator – Good capacity for doubt
punteggio.25-40: 📉 High risk – Review your information habits

questions.title: 📱 The viral post
questions.situation: You scroll your feed and see a post with 15,000 shares: "Tap water contains DISSIDIUM! They've been hiding it for years!" The linked article is from a .xyz domain and the author is 'Independent Researcher'.
questions.question: What is your instinctive reaction?

options.text: I check fact-checks on Open.europa or official pages before any action
options.explanation: ✅ Correct! Checking institutional and accredited fact-checking sources is your first line of defence. 'Dissidium' doesn't exist in chemistry – it's a made-up term that sounds scientific.

options.text: 15,000 shares mean there must be something true to it
options.explanation: ❌ Trap! Virality does not mean truth. Content that triggers fear spreads faster – that's exactly what manipulators rely on.

options.text: I share to warn friends and family about the 'danger'
options.explanation: ❌ Dangerous! Even with good intentions, you amplify disinformation. 68% of health hoaxes spread this way, through people trying to 'protect' others.

questions.bonus_insight: 💡 .xyz, .tk, .ml domains are often used for disinformation sites because they're free and lightly regulated.

questions.title: 🗳️ The TV debate
questions.situation: During an election debate, Candidate A says: 'My opponent ran three companies into the ground!' Candidate B replies: 'You evaded taxes!' Neither provides evidence or sources. The moderator doesn't intervene.
questions.question: How do you read this exchange?

options.text: If one of them were lying, the other would debunk it with evidence
options.explanation: ❌ Fallacious reasoning! In an unmoderated clash, both can lie freely. The absence of a rebuttal is not proof of truth.

options.text: These are mutual accusations with no evidence: a sign of propaganda, not debate
options.explanation: ✅ Well done! Recognising the lack of evidence is essential. Serious debate requires verifiable claims, not trading accusations.

options.text: I look for independent checks on both claims afterwards
options.explanation: 👍 Good practice! Not settling for the debate and checking afterwards is a sign of active critical thinking.

questions.bonus_insight: 💡 Studies show 73% of claims in political debates contain verifiable exaggerations or inaccuracies.

questions.title: 🌍 Two conflicting stories
questions.situation: Same day, same event (a climate protest): Outlet A headlines 'HISTORIC PROTEST: 500,000 IN THE STREETS!' while Outlet B writes 'TOTAL FLOP: a few thousand lost'. The photos are different.
questions.question: How do you approach this discrepancy?

options.text: I read both articles and verify the sources of the figures given
options.explanation: ✅ Excellent approach! Official figures come from police/city sources. Comparing primary sources often reveals the truth between opposing narratives.

options.text: One of them is deliberately lying
options.explanation: ⚠️ Too simplistic. Often it's not 'lying' but selective framing: different times, areas, sources. Reality is more nuanced.

options.text: I trust the outlet that usually reflects my views
options.explanation: ❌ Confirmation bias! This tendency feeds our information bubble: we only see what confirms what we already believe.

questions.bonus_insight: 💡 Photos can show different moments: an empty square at 8am doesn't disprove a full square at 2pm. Always check timestamps.

questions.title: 🔥 The article that makes you angry
questions.situation: You read an article with a shocking headline: 'The rich are stealing water from the poor! SCANDAL!' The piece has emotional testimonials, no numbers, and ends with 'Share to spread the truth!'
questions.question: What do you notice and what do you do?

options.text: Strong emotion is a signal: I pause for 10 minutes before any action
options.explanation: ✅ Recognising emotion as a 'warning bell' is the key move. Manipulators rely on anger to bypass critical thinking.

options.text: There's no concrete data and the call-to-action is suspicious
options.explanation: 👍 Noticing the lack of data and the pressure to share is good. Serious content doesn't ask for emotional shares.

options.text: I share: if it's true, people need to know now
options.explanation: ❌ Dangerous! The combo 'strong emotion + urgency + no data' is the trifecta of manipulation. Exactly the kind of content to double-check.

questions.bonus_insight: 💡 Manipulators use 'bait-and-switch': they anger you with a headline, then the body doesn't prove anything. But you've already shared.

questions.title: 🔬 The mysterious expert
questions.situation: A 5-minute video shows a man in a white coat explaining how 'official medicine is hiding the cure for cancer'. The channel has 2 million subscribers but the video gives no name, credentials, or institution.
questions.question: How do you rate this source?

options.text: 2 million subscribers mean someone has checked it
options.explanation: ❌ Critical error! Followers can be bought and algorithms reward engagement, not truth. A channel can be popular and completely unreliable.

options.text: White coat ≠ medical expertise: I want a name and verifiable credentials
options.explanation: ✅ Perfect! A white coat is a costume anyone can wear. A real medical expert always gives name, specialisation and institutional affiliation.

options.text: I read the comments to see if anyone has debunked it
options.explanation: ⚠️ Weak strategy. Comments can be manipulated, critics removed, bots used for fake consensus. The primary source stays anonymous.

questions.bonus_insight: 💡 The 'hidden cure' conspiracy has existed for every disease since the 1800s. If it were real, at least one researcher would have come forward for the Nobel.

questions.title: ⚔️ The personal attack
questions.situation: A politician presents a detailed tax reform with data and projections. An opponent responds: 'This guy was convicted of drunk driving 15 years ago! You trust him?'
questions.question: How do you analyse this criticism?

options.text: The attack on the person is irrelevant to the merit of the reform
options.explanation: 👍 Right direction! It's a classic ad hominem: attacking the speaker instead of what they say. Note that the conviction is real, unlike many political smears.

options.text: The past conviction makes me doubt their overall reliability
options.explanation: ⚠️ Understandable but fallacious. Even if the conviction is real, it doesn't automatically invalidate the tax proposal. Judge the content, not just the proposer.

options.text: It's an ad hominem that deflects from the real issue: the reform should be judged on its content
options.explanation: ✅ Excellent analysis! Recognising the personal attack as a diversion keeps you focused on the actual proposal.

questions.bonus_insight: 💡 Ad hominem can contain truth (the person may have real flaws) but it remains a fallacy when used to avoid discussing the argument.

questions.title: 📚 The 'irrefutable' video
questions.situation: A 90-minute video titled '100 proofs the government is lying' lists 100 alleged scandals, each covered in under a minute. No sources are cited, but 'it all fits' because the pieces seem to slot together.
questions.question: How do you approach this mass of 'proofs'?

options.text: So many claims = at least some are probably true
options.explanation: ❌ Gish Gallop trap! Flooding with claims makes it impossible to check everything. One true claim gives undue credibility to all the rest.

options.text: I verify 3–4 at random: if they're false, the rest is suspect
options.explanation: ✅ Smart strategy! Sampling lets you judge overall reliability. If 30–40% of checks fail, the whole thing is compromised.

options.text: I watch it all and then form a complete picture
options.explanation: ❌ Saturation risk! After 90 minutes of unsourced claims, the brain tends to accept them passively. That's exactly what the manipulator wants.

questions.bonus_insight: 💡 The 'flood of data' technique is used in trials too: overwhelm with weak evidence. Quantity is never quality.

questions.title: ⏰ Artificial urgency
questions.situation: A message is circulating in groups: 'SHARE NOW before they censor this! The government is about to pass a law BANNING growing vegetables at home!'
questions.question: How do you react to the urgency?

options.text: If it were true, it would be in every paper by tomorrow
options.explanation: ✅ Correct reasoning! Real news doesn't need 'pre-emptive censorship'. If a government passed such a law, it would be public and verifiable.

options.text: I share straight away: better safe than sorry
options.explanation: ❌ Exactly what the manipulator wants! Artificial urgency is there to bypass critical thinking. No serious law is passed in secret and enforced overnight.

options.text: I save the message and search for official news about the alleged law
options.explanation: 👍 Good practice! Saving instead of sharing is step one. Checking official sources (government gazette, official sites) is step two.

questions.bonus_insight: 💡 Urgency is the number one red flag. True information can wait for verification; false information needs your haste.

questions.title: 🎭 The 'ex' expert
questions.situation: A viral video shows an 'ex pharma executive' revealing disturbing industry secrets. The host introduces them as a 'courageous whistleblower'. There are no details on which company, when, or what role they had.
questions.question: How do you assess this testimony?

options.text: An 'ex' who speaks has more credibility because they risk backlash
options.explanation: ❌ Not necessarily! Without verifying who they were and when, anyone can claim to be 'ex something'. Real whistleblowers provide verifiable documents and names.

options.text: I want specific details: which company, which role, which years
options.explanation: ✅ Right approach! A real insider has an interest in documenting their position: it adds credibility without compromising safety.

options.text: I share it as a 'possible truth' to keep an eye on
options.explanation: ⚠️ Adds noise. Sharing unverified content as 'worth watching' still spreads potential falsehoods.

questions.bonus_insight: 💡 Real whistleblowers (like Edward Snowden or Frances Haugen) provide verifiable documents and accept legal consequences. Total anonymity is suspicious.

questions.title: 🧩 The perfect puzzle
questions.situation: A documentary links apparently unrelated events (a suspicious death, a private meeting, a share purchase) to prove a conspiracy. 'Too many coincidences to be random!' says the narrator.
questions.question: What do you make of this reasoning?

options.text: I verify each event on its own before accepting the connection
options.explanation: ✅ Scientific method in action! Each 'piece' must be verified independently. Often one turns out to be false and the whole construction collapses.

options.text: If the puzzle fits, only a few pieces are missing
options.explanation: ❌ Reasoning error! You can link unrelated events by building false narratives. Pattern matching is a very strong cognitive bias.

options.text: Coincidence isn't proof: I look for direct evidence of the link
options.explanation: 👍 Good approach! Correlation doesn't imply causation. A real conspiracy needs evidence of coordination, not just things happening at the same time.

questions.bonus_insight: 💡 Our brains are wired to find patterns. In a world of 8 billion people, 'incredible coincidences' happen every day by pure statistics.

0-8 punti.description: You have excellent critical thinking. You recognise manipulation techniques and don't let emotions override your judgement. Keep it up and help others develop the same awareness.
suggestions: Share your skills with friends and family
Stay updated on new disinformation techniques

9-16 punti.description: You have a good basis in critical thinking but some manipulation techniques can still catch you out. Your instinct is good; you could strengthen a few specific defences.
suggestions: Practise the '10 minute rule' before sharing
Build a list of reliable sources to check regularly

17-24 punti.description: You're vulnerable to some manipulation techniques, especially those that play on emotions. Don't worry: recognition is the first step to defence.
suggestions: Whenever you feel strong emotion online, pause
Never trust content without verifiable sources
Subscribe to one reliable outlet and compare with free sources

25-40 punti.description: Your defences against propaganda are weak. That doesn't mean you 'believe everything', but your information habits expose you to significant risk.
suggestions: Start by ALWAYS checking sources before sharing
Use fact-checking tools like Open.europa, Full Fact, Snopes

resources.name: Snopes
resources.description: Historical database of hoaxes and urban legends
